name: #74 — Follow Up - Association
subject: Checking in on your bee colony report.

content.text: Thank you for making a difference for honey bees.
Hi {{ params.reportername }},
Hopefully, by now, the beekeeper who claimed your report has been in touch, and things are moving along smoothly, whether they’re working on the rescue or the bees are already safe in their new home.
By reporting this colony, you’ve likely helped save an estimated
{{ params.numberofbees }} honey bees.
Thank you for turning to {{ params.beekeeperassociation }} to get these bees safely into a local beekeeper's hive.
If you have any feedback you'd like to share, please feel free to reply to this email.

name: #66 — Report Update - Wasps
subject: Update: Wasps, Not Bees - What You Need to Know

content.text: Your report has been identified as wasps, not bees.
A beekeeper has reviewed your report and identified that you're dealing with wasps, not bees.

Wasps (yellowjackets, hornets, etc.) can be more aggressive and pose different risks compared to honey bees. While bees are critical pollinators we aim to protect, wasps are generally only handled by pest control professionals, not beekeepers.
We encourage you to contact our trusted
local pest control partners
who can safely assess and assist you with the wasp removal.
Thank you for using Swarmed,
Mateo
Master Beekeeper and Managing Director of Swarmed
Spotting wasp nests
Wasps are most active during late summer and early fall when their colonies reach peak population. During this time, they become more aggressive in protecting their territory and searching for food.
Dealing with wasps
Unlike honey bees that are critical pollinators, wasps can pose risks to human safety. When wasps build nests near homes or high-traffic areas, professional removal may be necessary.
Wasp safety
Wasps are more aggressive than honey bees and can sting multiple times without dying. Their stings are not just painful, but can be dangerous for people with allergies.

name: #61 — Welcome Email Contributor
subject: A personal thanks for joining Swarmed

content.text: Dear {{ params.beekeepername }},
I’m Mateo, a fifth-generation beekeeper and master beekeeper. Thank you for joining our community of beekeepers!
I built Swarmed with one goal: to make it easier for people to report swarms and for beekeepers like us to respond quickly.

Thousands of beekeepers are now using Swarmed every day to catch swarms across North America and Australia. Plus, we're using the anonymous swarm data we collect to help advance honey bee health research. Here's a bit more about what we're doing:
Getting 10,000 swarms a year into beekeeper's hives
We're the only group out there running digital ads and working to game the search algorithms to get more swarm reports to you.
We also work with nonprofits, municipalities, libraries, emergency dispatch services, and
beekeeping associations
worldwide to get more swarms reported to beekeepers.
If you’d like to get more involved, you're also welcome to join our
Bee Swarm Catching Community
on Facebook,
follow Swarmed
, and help
spread the buzz
by sharing this service with other beekeepers and your wider community.
If you have any questions, check out our
FAQ/Wiki page
or feel free to reach out to me directly.
Thanks for joining!
Best,
Mateo

Your request to join {{ params.beekeeperassociation }} on Swarmed has been successfully shared with the group administrator(s).
We'll let you know once your request to join has been reviewed.

In the meantime, you'll still receive notifications of colonies reported through Swarmed. Once you're approved, you'll also receive notifications of colonies reported via {{ params.beekeeperassociation }}.
If you have questions about your request, please contact {{ params.beekeeperassociation }} at {{ params.beekeeperassociationemail }}

name: #23 — Showcase Email
subject: A bee swarm was just reported near you

content.text: A swarm has been reported in Woodland
1 mile away
Size: Basketball
Situation: On a tree/bush
Height: 5-10 ft
Duration: Less than 5 hours
For more details or to claim this swarm:
This swarm was reported via
Your Brand Here
Beekeeper Best Practices
If you capture the swarm, follow our best practices to make the experience smooth for both the bees and the person who reported them to you.
By claiming a swarm, you take responsibility for ensuring a safe and successful removal.
Don’t forget to check that your actions comply with all laws and regulations in your area.
Good luck!
-Mateo
Respond Promptly
When you claim a swarm, be sure to message the person promptly to make a plan to pick up the swarm so that they know they are in good hands.
Set Expectations
Swarm captures are typically free of charge unless the bees are in a hard-to-reach area or part of an established colony.
Safety First
Your safety, and that of those around you is your responsibility. Never attempt to capture swarms in unsafe locations.
Swarmed
info@beeswarmed.org

name: #22 — Reporter Final Follow Up
subject: One last note about bees

content.text: Did You Know?
It takes around
12 honey bees
their entire lifetimes to produce just
one teaspoon of honey
, visiting
tens of thousands of flowers
in the process. By rescuing swarms, we help preserve these incredible pollinators and the vital work they do for our ecosystems.

name: #18 — Follow Up - Feedback + Donate
subject: Checking in on your bee colony report.

content.text: Hi {{ params.reportername }},

I’m Mateo, a Master Beekeeper, and I run Swarmed. Hopefully, by now, the beekeeper who claimed your report has been in touch, and things are moving along smoothly, whether they’re working on the rescue or the bees are already safe in their new home.
By reporting this colony, you’ve likely helped save an estimated
{{ params.numberofbees }} honey bees.
Every year, millions of bee swarms emerge, but only
1 in 4 colonies survive without help
Swarmed exists to change that by connecting the public with beekeepers who can safely rescue and rehome bees.
Thank you for being part of this work. With your help, we’re saving honey bees, supporting beekeepers and bee researchers, and protecting the ecosystems we all depend on.
If you’d like to do more for bees, here are some quick things you can do:
Leave Us a Review
Thank you for making a difference for honey bees
Using Swarmed helps ensure a better future for our pollinators and the beekeeping community.
Best,
Mateo, Master Beekeeper & Managing Director of Swarmed

If the bees are still there, we recommend contacting your local beekeeping association to see if they can assist with your specific situation.
We’re sorry we couldn’t connect you with a beekeeper, but thank you for using Swarmed to help protect honey bees.

name: #14 — 24 Hours No Beekeeper Found
subject: No Beekeeper Available for Your Report

content.text: We’re Sorry – No Beekeeper Was Found
Unfortunately, it looks like none of the beekeepers in your area were able to claim your report this time.
If the bees are still there, we recommend:
Contacting your local beekeeping association
to see if they can assist with your specific situation.
Trying again on Swarmed
. Adding a clearer or closer photo of the swarm can often help attract a beekeeper, as it gives them a better sense of the situation.
We’re sorry we couldn’t connect you with a beekeeper right now, but thank you for using Swarmed to help protect honey bees.
This colony was reported via {{ params.swarmassociation }}

name: #13 — Swarmed Claimed + Swarm Status
subject: Your bee colony report has been claimed by a local beekeeper

content.text: Your Report Has Been Claimed by {{ params.beekeepername }}
If you don’t hear from {{ params.beekeepername }} soon, feel free to contact them at {{ params.beekeepercontact }}
If you ever want to switch beekeepers or update your report, just head to your Report Status page. From there, you can make the swarm available again for others to claim.
What to expect:
By reporting the colony to a local beekeeper, you’re ensuring these bees are safely relocated rather than exterminated.
This helps protect honey bee populations, which are essential to our environment and food systems.
Thank you for being part of this work!
Mateo
Managing Director, Swarmed
A Beekeeper Will Contact You Soon
Once a beekeeper claims your report, they’ll reach out to you directly to coordinate the safe removal of the colony. This might be through email, text, or phone.
Swarm Removals Are Often Free
Beekeepers often rescue swarms at no cost. However, if the bees are in a difficult-to-reach spot or involves special circumstances, they may quote you a price for their time and skill.
Your Safety Is a Priority
While waiting for the beekeeper to arrive, avoid disturbing the bees and keep a safe distance.

name: #11 — Report Confirmed + Report Status
subject: Thanks for reporting a bee colony!

content.text: Thank you for reporting a colony in {{ params.swarmcity }}
You'll receive an email from us as soon as a local beekeeper responds to your report.
Hi {{ params.reportername }}, this is Mateo, Master Beekeeper and Managing Director of Swarmed.
Thank you for reporting a bee colony and choosing to protect these vital pollinators. Your report helps us get one step closer to
our goal of protecting 200 million bees and pollinating 10 billion flowers a year
Swarmed is free to use, but if you’d like to support our work, we welcome a
$12 suggested contribution here
Thank you,
Mateo
This colony was reported via {{ params.swarmassociation }}
What to expect:
By reporting the colony through Swarmed, you’re ensuring these bees are safely relocated by a local beekeeper rather than exterminated.
This helps protect honey bee populations, which are essential to our environment and food systems.
Thank you for being part of this work!
-Mateo
A Beekeeper Will Contact You Soon
Once a beekeeper claims your report, they’ll reach out to you directly to coordinate the safe removal of the bees. This might be through email, text, or phone.
Swarm Removals Are Often Free
Beekeepers often rescue bee swarms at no cost. However, if the colony is in a difficult-to-reach spot or is an established hive, they may quote you a price for their time and skill.
Your Safety Is a Priority
While waiting for the beekeeper to arrive, avoid disturbing the bees and keep a safe distance.

If you capture the colony, follow our best practices to make the experience smooth for both the bees and the person who reported them to you.
By claiming a swarm or established hive, you take responsibility for ensuring a safe and successful removal.
Don’t forget to check that your actions comply with all laws and regulations in your area.
Good luck!

Respond Promptly

Set Expectations

Safety First
Your safety, and that of those around you is your responsibility. Never attempt to capture colonies in unsafe locations.

name: #7 — Beekeeper Skipped
subject: You've been skipped.

content.text: The report you recently claimed in {{ params.swarmcity }} has been released for other beekeepers to claim.
The individual who reported the bee colony has decided to proceed with a different responder and you are no longer responsible for this report.
This decision could be based on various factors, such as response time, proposed handling methods, or other criteria set by the person who reported the colony.
The report has been made available to other beekeepers in your area. To avoid confusion, please refrain from contacting the colony reporter again.
If you have any questions or concerns feel free to send us a message.

name: #6 — Welcome Email Association Follow Up Invite
subject: Swarmed for Midwest Beekeepers Association

content.text: Hi {{ params.beekeepername }},
Thanks again for signing up for Swarmed. I noticed that you're a part of {{ params.beekeeperassociation }}.
I wanted to reach out to you because joining Swarmed with your association is the best way to get more swarms reported in your area and help your group thrive.
To see how Swarmed integrates with associations from Hawaii to New Hampshire to make swarm reporting easier, faster, and more private, you can visit our
Partnerships
page. I also wanted to send you a presentation about Swarmed for Associations that you're more than welcome to share with your association. You can find that
here
Thanks,
Mateo

name: #5 — Welcome Email + Contribute
subject: A personal thanks for joining Swarmed

content.text: Dear {{ params.beekeepername }},
I’m Mateo, a fifth-generation beekeeper and certified master beekeeper. Thank you for joining our community of thousands of beekeepers responding to swarms!
We're the only beekeepers out there using search algorithms to get more swarms to you, not exterminators.
We also work with nonprofits, municipalities, libraries, emergency dispatch services, and
beekeeping associations
worldwide to get more swarms reported to beekeepers.
Want to learn more about how Swarmed works? I sat down with Fred Dunn to walk through exactly what we're building for beekeepers. You can watch that conversation
here
If you’d like to get more involved, you're also welcome to join our
Bee Swarm Catching Community
on Facebook, and help
spread the buzz
by sharing this service with other beekeepers and your wider community.
If you have any questions, check out our
FAQ/Wiki page
or feel free to reach out to me directly.
Thanks again for joining!
Best,
Mateo from Swarmed
Swarmed depends on the support of beekeepers through pay-what-you-want contributions.
Please consider supporting our work by clicking the link below or going logging in and going to
Settings > Manage Plan
